FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 3.0 with 11,878 participants aged 9-10 years at baseline and 6,571 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures recreational screen time via Youth Screen Time Survey (Bagot et al., 2018), reported as average hours per weekday/weekend and weekly screen time

FT_IC3.evidence: Bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models with inferential tests linking lifestyle risk factor domain (including screen time) to brain volume domains (correlation Φ, path γ2). Multiple statistically significant pathways reported (e.g., temporal-parietal: β = −0.134, p = .002)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients, z-scores, and p-values reported in Table 2. Example: temporal-parietal baseline covariance β = −0.197 (0.026), Z = −7.661, p < .001

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using longitudinal latent change score modeling on cross-sectional and 2-year follow-up ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Health Psychology, 2023. Not identified as duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is one component of a clustered lifestyle risk factor domain (also includes sleep, physical activity, BMI). Analyses include cross-sectional associations and screen time as a predictor of brain volume changes (not as a dependent variable only). Therefore FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: E3: Latent construct — screen time is part of a latent lifestyle risk factor domain with separately extractable pathway to brain volume outcomes, MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study with participants aged 9-12 years from first two years of the study; ABCD Data Release 4.0 mentioned

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures self-reported digital screen media activity (SMA) including time spent on six digital activities: TV shows/movies, online videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat. Six-item 7-point scale for time spent per activity

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) testing relationships between screen media activity profiles and functional brain organization (resting state fMRI connectivity). Group factor analysis (GFA) with GAMM models examining correlations between digital screen engagement and brain network connectivity

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression statistics reported including factor loadings and p-values. GFA groupings explained variance (GFA grouping 1: 9.34% of variance; GFA grouping 2: 8.56% of total variance)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using registered report protocol, cross-sectional and exploratory longitudinal analyses of screen media activity and functional brain organization

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Cortex, 2023. Registered report. Not identified as duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is examined as a predictor of functional brain organization (brain connectivity), not as a dependent variable. Cross-sectional and exploratory longitudinal analyses are included. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: E4: ML/GAMM — coefficients and p-values reported for GAMM models examining screen media activity associations with functional brain networks. Results did not support hypotheses that SMA relates to functional brain organization or maladaptive functioning.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from year 2 (September 10, 2018 to September 29, 2020). Analysis based on 5,797 adolescents aged 10-14 years from 21 racially and ethnically diverse study sites across the US

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures self-reported recreational screen time categorized into 3 levels: low (0-4 hours per day), medium (>4-8 hours per day), high (>8 hours per day) from ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression models and logistic regression models testing associations between screen time categories and BMI percentile/overweight and obesity. Multiple statistical tests reported with risk ratios and odds ratios

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk ratios (RR) and 95% confidence intervals reported. Example: medium screen time RR = 1.24 (95% CI, 1.12-1.37); high screen time RR = 1.29 (95% CI, 1.16-1.44) for overweight or obesity

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis examining associations between screen time and physical activity categories with BMI outcome

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, JAMA Network Open, 2023. Not identified as duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is examined as an independent predictor of BMI outcome (not as dependent variable). Multiple predictors tested including physical activity. Cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: E8: Inferential tests (Poisson regression, logistic regression) with reported coefficients (RR, OR, CI) testing screen time associations with BMI and overweight/obesity, not narrative-only.
